content.text: A demo or showcase turns your agent from code into an experience. It is the difference between 'I built a research agent' — a claim — and 'here, try it' — a demonstration. For portfolio purposes, a well-constructed demo is sometimes more persuasive than the underlying code, because it shows that the system actually works on inputs the reviewer did not choose.
A demo or showcase for an agent project is a live or recorded presentation of the agent working on real inputs. It may take several forms:
a hosted web application (Gradio, Streamlit, or a custom frontend) where a visitor can type a real input and watch the agent work.
a screen recording or Loom video showing a representative agent run, narrated to explain what is happening and why.
a structured display of saved agent traces — the full sequence of LLM calls, tool calls, and intermediate outputs for a representative run.
a Jupyter or Colab notebook that runs the agent on a curated set of inputs with outputs pre-rendered in cells.

Each format reaches a different audience and serves a different purpose. A live demo impresses technical reviewers and non-technical stakeholders alike. A trace showcase speaks to engineers who want to understand the agent's reasoning. A recorded walkthrough is the most accessible format for sharing widely.

Code repositories are passive — they wait to be read and understood. A demo is active — it shows the agent working. For a hiring manager, a two-minute video of an agent successfully completing a research task is more persuasive than an hour of reading code.

Demos also serve as proof against a common skepticism about AI projects: 'does it actually work on real inputs, not just the cherry-picked examples in the README?' A live demo that the reviewer can probe with their own inputs addresses this skepticism directly.
a live demo or video requires no setup, no code reading, and no domain expertise to evaluate.
a live demo that accepts the reviewer's own input proves the system is not fragile or narrowly overfit to examples in the README.
a Gradio or Streamlit link can be shared in a Slack message or LinkedIn post and viewed immediately without any installation.
a live demo that visitors can try generates feedback, questions, and word-of-mouth that a static README cannot.
building a demo forces you to harden the agent against diverse inputs, improving the project's overall quality.
a live demo on HuggingFace Spaces, Railway, or Render costs money or is subject to sleep timers that make the first load slow.
every visitor to a live demo costs you API money. Without spending caps and rate limiting, a viral post can drain your budget.
live demos break. Models change, APIs deprecate, hosting environments drift. A broken demo is worse than no demo.
a live demo exposes an LLM endpoint to the internet. Prompt injection, abuse, and credential leakage are real risks.
a demo that only shows the happy path can backfire if the reviewer tries an edge case and the agent fails visibly.
Building a demo involves three layers beyond the agent itself:
the interface the user interacts with. Gradio requires minimal code for a chat-style or input/output demo. Streamlit offers more layout control. A custom HTML frontend gives full design freedom.
the agent is typically served as an HTTP endpoint (FastAPI or Flask) that the UI calls. This separation lets you update the agent without rebuilding the UI.
rate limiting, input length limits, spending caps, and output filtering. These are not optional for a public demo.

attrs.alt: Agent demo architecture: user interacts with demo UI layer (Gradio/Streamlit), which calls a rate-limited agent API, which calls the LLM layer (cached, spending-capped) and tools layer (sandboxed)

content.text: A production-quality demo has these components:
a clean, minimal UI that makes the agent's capability obvious without explaining it. A single text input and a structured output display is often enough.
a FastAPI or similar server that receives requests, enforces rate limits, and calls the agent.
the same agent code from your portfolio project, unchanged. The demo is a wrapper, not a rewrite.
a per-day or per-hour spending cap that stops the demo if the budget is exceeded. Log when this triggers.
an optional fallback that returns pre-computed responses when the live API is unavailable or the budget is exhausted. Prevents a blank screen.
Your agent produces output that is visually interesting (reports, summaries, structured data, generated code) and benefits from being seen working.
You are sharing the project with non-technical stakeholders (recruiters, product managers) who cannot evaluate code.
You want to generate feedback from a broader audience than your immediate network.
You have a specific showcase context — a talk, a meetup, a blog post — where a live demo would be more compelling than screenshots.

# Minimal Gradio demo for a research agent
import gradio as gr
import anthropic
import os

client = anthropic.Anthropic(api_key=os.environ["ANTHROPIC_API_KEY"])

# Simple per-session rate limiting
from collections import defaultdict
from time import time

request_counts = defaultdict(list)

def rate_limit(session_id: str, max_per_hour: int = 5) -> bool:
    now = time()
    requests = [t for t in request_counts[session_id] if now - t < 3600]
    request_counts[session_id] = requests
    if len(requests) >= max_per_hour:
        return False
    request_counts[session_id].append(now)
    return True

def run_demo(question: str, request: gr.Request) -> str:
    session_id = str(request.client.host)
    if not rate_limit(session_id):
        return "Rate limit reached. Please try again in an hour."
    if len(question) > 500:
        return "Input too long. Please limit to 500 characters."

    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5",   # use the cheapest model for demos
        max_tokens=1024,
        system="You are a helpful research assistant. Answer concisely.",
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": question}]
    )
    return response.content[0].text

demo = gr.Interface(
    fn=run_demo,
    inputs=gr.Textbox(label="Your research question", placeholder="e.g. What is chain-of-thought prompting?"),
    outputs=gr.Markdown(label="Agent Response"),
    title="Research Agent Demo",
    description="Ask a research question. The agent searches for relevant information and synthesizes an answer.",
    examples=[
        ["What are the main approaches to multi-agent coordination?"],
        ["Explain the difference between RAG and fine-tuning."],
    ],
    allow_flagging="never"
)

demo.launch(server_name="0.0.0.0", server_port=7860)
Demo formats for common agent types:
chat interface where user enters a question, agent streams intermediate steps (search queries, retrieved snippets) and final answer in real time.
split-pane UI showing user's bug description on the left and the agent's trace (file reads, proposed fix, test run result) on the right.
file upload interface where user uploads a CSV, agent generates and runs analysis code, and returns a formatted report with key insights.
a visual flow diagram showing which agent is currently active, with a real-time log of messages between agents.
a static HTML page showing 5 representative traces with inputs, reasoning steps, tool calls, and outputs formatted for readability.

HuggingFace Spaces hosts thousands of agent demos built with Gradio. A well-built Spaces demo gets discovered through the platform's gallery, accumulates likes, and generates organic sharing on social media. The best-performing demos typically have:
A clear, single-sentence description of what the agent does.
Two to four curated example inputs in the Gradio examples panel, so visitors can run a demo immediately without having to think of an input.
Streaming output that shows the agent working in real time rather than a blank screen followed by a full response.
A 'how this works' accordion section that explains the agent's architecture without requiring the visitor to read the code.
the first time your demo link is shared, it will get more traffic than expected. Rate limiting is not optional.
Using the most powerful (and expensive) model for the demo:
use a smaller, faster, cheaper model for demos. The goal is to show capability, not to use the best model.
if your demo depends entirely on a live API and the API is down, visitors see an error. Pre-compute representative outputs as a fallback.
a demo that shows too many options, too many settings, or too much raw JSON output confuses visitors. Show the interesting output, hide the machinery.
'enter a research question' is intimidating. Pre-populate the input box with a concrete example. Add 3–5 examples the visitor can click to run immediately.
streaming shows the agent working in real time and is far more engaging than a blank wait followed by a full response.
display the agent's tool calls, search queries, or reasoning steps alongside the final output. This is the most differentiating feature of an agent demo versus a simple chatbot.
maintain a 60-second screen recording of a demo run. If the live demo is down, you can share the video.
curated examples show the agent's capability ceiling, not just the average case.
Use a custom domain for the demo:
myprojecto.ai looks more intentional than a random HuggingFace Spaces URL.
check your demo's usage logs weekly. Unusual patterns (many long requests, repeated identical inputs) may indicate automated abuse.
For a demo that needs to handle sudden traffic spikes (e.g., after a blog post or social media share):
cache LLM responses for identical or near-identical inputs. The same example queries get run repeatedly by different visitors.
streaming reduces perceived latency significantly. A visitor watching a response stream in real time is more patient than one staring at a spinner.
containerize your demo (Docker) so it can be replicated if traffic spikes. Platforms like Railway and Render support this.
set up email or SMS alerts when your API spending crosses a threshold so you can take the demo offline before costs spiral.
much lower cost to maintain but much less persuasive. Screenshots can be fabricated; live demos cannot.
excellent for showing code alongside output but requires the visitor to run it themselves. Use Google Colab for zero-installation access.
low maintenance but passive. Visitors cannot probe the agent with their own inputs.
eliminates cost and security concerns but limits your audience to people who can set up a local environment.
A public demo is an attack surface. Protect it:

any text input can attempt to override the system prompt. Include explicit instructions in the system prompt about ignoring attempts to change behavior, and monitor outputs for obvious injection successes.
enforce a maximum input length (e.g., 500 characters for a demo) to prevent token-stuffing attacks.
if your demo might produce sensitive content, add output filtering before displaying to the user.
use a dedicated API key for your demo, separate from your personal development key. If the demo key is compromised, you can rotate it without affecting other projects.
if you run a separate frontend and backend, configure CORS to allow only your frontend's origin.

A demo is a product, not a debug session. When you build the demo, wear the product hat: what does the user experience from the moment they land on the page to the moment they see the output? Every friction point — a confusing label, a slow load, a blank spinner — is a reason for the visitor to leave before seeing the agent work.

The demo's job is to make the agent's capability obvious in under 60 seconds. If a visitor has to read three paragraphs to understand what the agent does, the demo has failed. If they see a result and immediately think 'I want to try my own input', it has succeeded.
A live demo proves the agent works on real inputs, not just cherry-picked examples — this directly addresses the most common skepticism about AI portfolio projects.
Always implement rate limiting, input length limits, and spending caps before making a demo public.
Use a cheaper, faster model for demos; the goal is to show capability, not to use the most powerful model.
Show intermediate steps (tool calls, search queries, reasoning) alongside the final output — this is the differentiating feature of an agent demo.
Maintain a backup video recording for when the live demo is unavailable.
Treat the demo as a product: minimize friction, pre-populate examples, and stream output to reduce perceived latency.

A trace showcase is an underused but highly effective portfolio format. Instead of (or in addition to) a live demo, you publish a curated set of saved agent traces — the complete sequence of system prompt, user message, LLM response, tool calls, tool results, and final output — formatted for readability.

Traces showcase something a live demo often cannot: the agent's reasoning process at each step. A reviewer can follow exactly how the agent decided to search for additional information, how it interpreted a tool result, and how it synthesized the final answer. This level of transparency is extremely compelling for technical evaluators.

# Script to format and save a trace as a readable HTML showcase
import json, html
from anthropic import Anthropic
from datetime import datetime

client = Anthropic()
trace = []

def traced_agent(user_message: str) -> str:
    messages = [{"role": "user", "content": user_message}]
    trace.append({"type": "user", "content": user_message, "ts": datetime.now().isoformat()})
    
    while True:
        response = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-opus-4-5", max_tokens=2048,
            tools=[...],  # your tool definitions
            messages=messages
        )
        for block in response.content:
            if block.type == "text":
                trace.append({"type": "assistant_text", "content": block.text})
            elif block.type == "tool_use":
                trace.append({"type": "tool_call", "name": block.name, "input": block.input})
        
        if response.stop_reason == "end_turn":
            return response.content[-1].text
        
        # handle tool calls, append tool results to trace...
        
# After running: save trace to JSON
import pathlib
pathlib.Path("traces/trace_001.json").write_text(json.dumps(trace, indent=2))

Publish 3–5 curated traces, each demonstrating a different capability or handling a different edge case. A simple HTML page that renders each trace as an accordion (click to expand each step) is an extremely effective portfolio artifact.

Well-executed demos have directly led to job offers for practitioners in the agentic AI space. The mechanism is usually: engineer shares a demo link in a Discord or Slack, the demo impresses someone in the community, that person DMs the engineer to ask how it works, a conversation starts, and within weeks there is an offer or referral.

To maximize this path, share your demo in the right communities at the right time. A new demo is most shareable in the first two weeks after launch. Share it in relevant Discord servers (LangChain, AutoGen, AI Tinkerers), in relevant subreddits (r/MachineLearning, r/LocalLLaMA for local model demos), and as a post on LinkedIn and X. Tag or mention maintainers of frameworks you used (appropriately — do not spam).

The demo should stand alone, but a short write-up alongside it (a post explaining how you built it and what you learned) extends the shareable surface area: some people click demos, others prefer to read technical write-ups. Together they reach both audiences.
"A live demo that accepts the reviewer's own input is the closest thing to a technical interview you can conduct asynchronously — at scale, for free, while you sleep."
